revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Sanjin''' (住吉三神, すみよしさんじん) is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命). They are sometimes referred to as the ''Sumiyoshi Daijin'' (住吉大神), but this name also includes [[Empress Jingū]] who is enshrined at [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. The Sumiyoshi Sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing.
[[de:Drei Sumiyoshi-Kami]] [[ja:住吉三神]]

'''Sumiyoshi Sanjin''' (住吉三神, すみよしさんじん) is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命). They are sometimes referred to as the ''Sumiyoshi Daijin'' (住吉大神), but this name also includes [[Empress Jingū]] who is enshrined at [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. The Sumiyoshi Sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing.
There are around 600 shrines around Japan that enshrine the Sumiyoshi Sanjin. [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[Osaka]] and Sumiyoshi Jinja in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]] city, [[Yamaguchi prefecture]], are two such shrines.
[[de:Drei Sumiyoshi-Kami]] [[ja:住吉三神]]

'''Sumiyoshi Sanjin''' (住吉三神, すみよしさんじん) is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命). They are sometimes referred to as the ''Sumiyoshi Daijin'' (住吉大神), but this name also includes [[Empress Jingū]] who is enshrined at [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. The Sumiyoshi Sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing.
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi Sanjin were born together with the [[Watatsumi Sanjin]] (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony.
Originally the Sumiyoshi Sanjin and Watasumi Sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi Sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi Sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi Sanjin moved to Kinki.
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi Sanjin the oldest are ''Sumiyoshi Jinja'' in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[Fukuoka prefecture]], ''Sumiyoshi Jinja'' in [[Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''Moto Sumiyoshi Jinja'' in [[Kobe]], [[Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory suggests the Sumiyoshi Sanjin are the diefication of the three main stars in the [[Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]] city, Tsutsuki in [[Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three Orion stars.
[[de:Drei Sumiyoshi-Kami]] [[ja:住吉三神]]

{{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
The [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]'' -- the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watasumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''Sumiyoshi jinja'' in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[Fukuoka prefecture]], ''Sumiyoshi jinja'' in [[Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''Moto Sumiyoshi jinja'' in [[Kobe]], [[Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], Tsutsuki in [[Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three Orion stars.
==Notes== {{reflist}}

The [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watasumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.

In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], Tsutsuki in [[Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three Orion stars.

However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], Tsutsuki in [[Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watasumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[Fukuoka prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[Moto Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[Kobe]], [[Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], Tsutsuki in [[Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[Fukuoka prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[Moto Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[Kobe]], [[Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], Tsutsuki in [[Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|qid=Q813858}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1114013]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi sanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in [[:en:Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[:en:Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].

The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1114013]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi sanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in [[:en:Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[:en:Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}

The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1114013]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi sanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in [[:en:Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[:en:Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1114013]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi sanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in [[:en:Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[:en:Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1114013]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi sanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in [[:en:Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[:en:Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.

According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]].
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|Hakata-ku]], [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki]] city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in [[:en:Itoshima District, Fukuoka|Itoshima]], Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three [[:en:Orion's Belt|Orion stars]].
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]] where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]] ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

(2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>

==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1114013]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi sanjin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}}.
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|WD=Q1330839|lt=Hakata-ku}}, [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|WD=Q861519|lt=Iki}} city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in {{ill|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1132262|lt=Itoshima}}, Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three {{ill|Orion's Belt|en|Orion's Belt|WD=Q324312|lt=Orion stars}}.
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji}} where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and {{ill|Hikaru Genji|en|Hikaru Genji|WD=Q997646|lt=Hikaru Genji}} ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}}.
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|WD=Q1330839|lt=Hakata-ku}}, [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|WD=Q861519|lt=Iki}} city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in {{ill|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1132262|lt=Itoshima}}, Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three {{ill|Orion's Belt|en|Orion's Belt|WD=Q324312|lt=Orion stars}}.
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji}} where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and {{ill|Hikaru Genji|en|Hikaru Genji|WD=Q997646|lt=Hikaru Genji}} ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}
{{jmyth navbox long}}]] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Sumiyoshi Faith}}

(2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}.
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|WD=Q1330839|lt=Hakata-ku|1=Hakata-ku, Fukuoka}}, [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|WD=Q861519|lt=Iki|1=Iki, Nagasaki}} city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in {{ill|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1132262|lt=Itoshima|1=Itoshima District, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three {{ill|Orion's Belt|en|Orion's Belt|WD=Q324312|lt=Orion stars|1=Orion's Belt}}.
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji|1=The Tale of Genji}} where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and {{ill|Hikaru Genji|en|Hikaru Genji|WD=Q997646|lt=Hikaru Genji|1=Hikaru Genji}} ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]

Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|WD=Q1330839|lt=Hakata-ku|1=Hakata-ku, Fukuoka}}, [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|WD=Q861519|lt=Iki|1=Iki, Nagasaki}} city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in {{ill|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1132262|lt=Itoshima|1=Itoshima District, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three {{ill|Orion's Belt|en|Orion's Belt|WD=Q324312|lt=Orion stars|1=Orion's Belt}}.
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji|1=The Tale of Genji}} where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and {{ill|Hikaru Genji|en|Hikaru Genji|WD=Q997646|lt=Hikaru Genji|1=Hikaru Genji}} ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Sumiyoshi-sanjin}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sumiyoshi-sanjin]][[de:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[en:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[it:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[ja:住吉三神]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯃꯤꯌꯣꯁꯤ ꯁꯟꯖꯤꯟ]][[my:ဆုမိရောရှိနတ်သုံးပါး]][[pl:Sumiyoshi Sanjin]][[simple:Sumiyoshi sanjin]][[zh:住吉三神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}}} {{Short description|Shinto gods of the sea and sailing}} {{nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi sanjin'''|住吉三神|}} is the generic name for the three [[Shinto]] gods ''Sokotsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (底筒男命), ''Nakatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (中筒男命), and ''Uwatsutsu no O no Mikoto'' (表筒男命).<ref>Sesko, Markus. (2011). {{Google books|kFTNe-WjyBcC|''Legends and Stories Around the Japanese Sword,'' p. 75 n.1.|page=75}}</ref> The Sumiyoshi sanjin are regarded as the gods of the sea and sailing. They are sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|''Sumiyoshi daijin''|住吉大神|}}.
The [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] has four buildings dedicated to four ''[[kami]]''—the three Sumiyoshi brothers and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} who is also enshrined.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sumiyoshi taisha (or 'jinja)" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 913|page=913}}.</ref>
According to Japanese mythology as written in works such as the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], the Sumiyoshi sanjin were born together with the {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} Sanjin (綿津見三神) when [[Izanagi]] performed a purification ceremony after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}.
Originally the Sumiyoshi sanjin and Watatsumi sanjin were the same gods, but when, in ancient times, the throne was moved east from [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to the area now known as [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]], it was the Sumiyoshi sanjin that supposedly served an important role. In other words, the Watatsumi sanjin stayed in Kyūshū and the Sumiyoshi sanjin moved to Kinki.{{Dubious|date=November 2023|reason=I think this is assuming the [[Yamatai Kyushu Theory]]}}
The three Watatsumis are speculated to correspond to three historical fishing practices Uwatsu Watatsumi no Kami (sea surface god), Nakatsu Watatsumi no Kami (middle depth god) and Sokotsu Watatsumi no Kami (depths god).<ref name=":023">{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|xxviii}}
Of the Shinto Shrines that enshrine the Sumiyoshi sanjin the oldest are ''[[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|WD=Q1330839|lt=Hakata-ku|1=Hakata-ku, Fukuoka}}, [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] city, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, ''[[Sumiyoshi jinja (Nagasaki)|Sumiyoshi jinja]]'' in {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|WD=Q861519|lt=Iki|1=Iki, Nagasaki}} city, [[Nagasaki prefecture]], and ''[[:en:Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine|Moto Sumiyoshi Shrine]]'' in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, [[:en:Hyōgo prefecture|Hyōgo prefecture]]. However, it is not known which one of these is the oldest.
The ''tsutsu'' part of the names of the three gods has a connection to the planets, and there is a theory that suggests the Sumiyoshi sanjin are the deification of the three main stars in the [[:en:Orion constellation|Orion constellation]]. In the olden days the Orion constellation was used for navigational purposes so it was perhaps for this reason that they were deified. Also, the locations of Tsutsu on [[Tsushima Island]], Tsutsuki on [[Iki Island]], and Tsutsuki in {{ill|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1132262|lt=Itoshima|1=Itoshima District, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka prefecture are in the arrangement of these three {{ill|Orion's Belt|en|Orion's Belt|WD=Q324312|lt=Orion stars|1=Orion's Belt}}.
== Appearances == Probably the most notable appearance is in {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji|1=The Tale of Genji}} where it is the luck deity which the father of the Akashi Lady prays to help his daughter find a husband and secure her well-being. The father, who is the Novitiate of the Province of Akashi, hence the name "Akashi Lady", devoutly prays to Sumiyoshi for many years and eventually Sumiyoshi grants his prayers and {{ill|Hikaru Genji|en|Hikaru Genji|WD=Q997646|lt=Hikaru Genji|1=Hikaru Genji}} ends up taking the Akashi Lady and giving her a daughter of The Akashi Princess, who eventually becomes Empress.

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128]
